note_data.title: Forces and matter
keyPoints: The **extension** of a spring is directly proportional to the applied force (provided the elastic limit is not exceeded)
**Hooke's law** states that force is proportional to extension: F = kx
The **spring constant** (k) is the force per unit extension and has units N/m
Beyond the **elastic limit**, the spring will not return to its original length when the force is removed
**Pressure** is force per unit area and is measured in pascals (Pa) or N/m²
Pressure in liquids increases with **depth** because of the weight of liquid above
The **limit of proportionality** is the point beyond which Hooke's law no longer applies
A stiffer spring has a **larger spring constant** and extends less for the same applied force

definitions.definition: The increase in length of a spring or wire when a force is applied
The force per unit extension of a spring, measured in N/m
The force acting per unit area, measured in pascals (Pa)
The maximum force that can be applied to a spring before it becomes permanently deformed
The extension of a spring is directly proportional to the applied force, provided the elastic limit is not exceeded

howToAnswer.instruction: Give one fact in one sentence. No explanation needed.
Give the point + reason using 'because' or 'this is because'
Show working clearly, include units in final answer
Say what happens step by step, use data from graphs/tables

modelAnswer.question: A spring extends by 5.0 cm when a force of 10 N is applied. Calculate the spring constant and determine the extension when a force of 25 N is applied.
modelAnswer.answer: Spring constant k = F/x = 10 N / 0.05 m = 200 N/m. When F = 25 N, extension x = F/k = 25 N / 200 N/m = 0.125 m = 12.5 cm
markBreakdown: Mark 3: Final answer 12.5 cm with unit

commonMistakes: Never forget to convert cm to metres when calculating - always use SI base units
Students often confuse elastic limit with limit of proportionality - elastic limit is about permanent deformation
Don't say 'pressure increases because of more water' - say 'pressure increases with depth due to the weight of liquid above'
Never use F = kx beyond the elastic limit - Hooke's law only applies within the elastic region
Students forget that spring constant has units N/m, not just a number
examinerTips: Force-extension graphs appear frequently - know the shape and key points
Always show clear working for pressure and spring calculations
Draw neat, labelled diagrams for spring experiments
Remember to state 'provided elastic limit not exceeded' when mentioning Hooke's law
note_data.keyDiagramDescription: Force-extension graph showing linear region (Hooke's law), limit of proportionality, elastic limit, and plastic deformation region. Spring experiment setup with masses, ruler, and clamp stand.
pastPaperRefs: This topic was tested in M/J 2023 P2 Q4 (3 marks) — they asked students to identify the limit of proportionality, elastic deformation, and plastic deformation regions on a force-extension graph for a wire
This topic appeared in O/N 2022 P1 Q15 (1 mark) — students had to determine the type of deformation when a force-extension graph shows the same line for loading and unloading
This topic was examined in M/J 2024 P2 Q3 (4 marks) — candidates needed to calculate specific extensions when weights were attached to springs, testing Hooke's Law applications
This topic featured in O/N 2023 P2 Q5 (2 marks) — students were required to mark and label the limit of proportionality on a given force-extension graph
This topic was tested in M/J 2022 P1 Q12 (1 mark) — examining spring constant calculations and maximum extension in oscillatory motion

workedExample.question: A spring has a spring constant of 25 N/m. When a mass of 0.40 kg is attached and the system reaches equilibrium, calculate: (a) the extension of the spring (b) the elastic potential energy stored. (4 marks)
steps: Step 1: Identify the forces at equilibrium. Weight = mg = 0.40 × 10 = 4.0 N
Step 2: Apply Hooke's Law. At equilibrium, spring force = weight, so F = kx, therefore 4.0 = 25 × x
Step 3: Calculate extension. x = F/k = 4.0/25 = 0.16 m = 16 cm
Step 4: Calculate elastic potential energy. E = ½kx² = ½ × 25 × (0.16)² = 0.32 J
workedExample.answer: (a) Extension = 0.16 m or 16 cm (b) Elastic potential energy = 0.32 J
workedExample.markScheme: 1 mark for correct weight calculation, 1 mark for correct application of Hooke's Law, 1 mark for extension answer with units, 1 mark for elastic potential energy calculation

examinerWarnings: Examiner Report 2023: 'Many candidates confused the limit of proportionality with the elastic limit' — always remember the limit of proportionality is where Hooke's Law stops working, while elastic limit is where permanent deformation begins
Examiner Report 2024: 'Students frequently forgot to convert units when reading values from graphs' — always check if extensions are given in mm or cm and convert to metres for calculations

Examiner Report 2022: 'Candidates often drew force-extension graphs with curves from the origin' — remember that Hooke's Law produces a straight line through the origin up to the limit of proportionality
note_data.keyDiagram: Force-extension graph with x-axis labeled 'Extension (m)' and y-axis labeled 'Force (N)'. Draw a straight line from origin showing elastic deformation region, mark point P where line curves (limit of proportionality), continue with curved line showing plastic deformation, and mark point Q where material breaks. Include labels for 'elastic deformation', 'plastic deformation', 'limit of proportionality', and 'breaking point'.
note_data.quickRecap: Hooke's Law (F = kx) applies only up to the limit of proportionality where the force-extension graph is linear. Beyond this point, materials undergo plastic deformation and won't return to original length. Elastic potential energy is stored as E = ½kx². Spring constant k represents stiffness - higher k means stiffer spring requiring more force for same extension.
